Furthermore, it made me feel very imaginative (富于想象的) as a kid. Actually, the wood was incredibly durable (耐用的), so it never broke. Consequently, it became my absolute pride and joy (掌上明珠 / 最得意的珍宝). Moreover, it reminds me of my prosperous (富裕的) and happy childhood. Overall, this authentic (真实的) memory is very precious (珍贵的) to me. Finally, playing with it was simple child's play (小儿科 / 极容易的事), making me feel deeply nostalgic (怀旧的) today.

q2.html:
Describe a gift you received as a child that you still remember. You should say:
What the gift was
Who gave it to you
When you received it
And explain why this gift is still important to you.

Honestly, I want to talk about a beautiful picture book that I received as a child. To begin with, the book was full of colorful illustrations and short fairy tales. Actually, my grandma gave it to me, because she wanted me to fall in love with reading from a young age. I still remember it clearly because she gave it to me on my sixth birthday, wrapped in shiny paper. To be honest, I still read it sometimes when I feel stressed. In the end, this simple gift means so much because it reminds me of her endless love and kindness.

q3.html:
Describe something old that your family has kept for a long time. You should say:
What the item is
How long your family has had it
Why your family keeps it
And explain how you feel about this item.

To begin with, I would like to describe an old wooden clock that hangs on the wall in our living room. Actually, my family has owned this clock for over forty years, since my grandfather bought it shortly after he got married. Honestly, my parents keep it because it represents an important part of our family history and reminds us of our roots. The clock still works perfectly, which is quite amazing. In the end, I feel very proud whenever I look at it, because it connects different generations together and shows how much my family values tradition and meaningful memories.

q1.html:
Q1: Do boys and girls prefer different kinds of toys?

Op Traditionally, yes, but modern parents are encouraging more gender-neutral options. Re Historically, society pushed dolls for girls and action figures for boys to shape their early socialization (社会化). Ex Today, educational building blocks are equally popular for both genders' development (发展).

q2.html:
Q2: How do toys that were popular in the past compare with toys that are popular now?

Op Undeniably, older toys were simpler, while modern toys are highly digital. Re Consequently, classic wooden toys required more physical interaction (互动) compared to today’s screen-based games. Ex For example, playing board games demands more face-to-face communication than solitary mobile applications.

q3.html:
Q3: What should people consider when choosing a toy for a child?

Op Primarily, safety is crucial, but educational value is also highly important. Re Specifically, a good toy should stimulate cognitive (认知的) growth and spark creativity (创造力). Ex For instance, puzzles are excellent because they teach children how to play by the rules (遵守规则) safely.

q4.html:
Q4: Should kids be permitted to pick their own toys at a toy store?

Op Occasionally, it is fine, provided parents set a strict budget beforehand. Re Otherwise, children might become a spoiled brat (被宠坏的孩子) if they always get whatever they demand. Ex As an illustration, allowing them to choose a small reward teaches them about the economy (经济 / 金钱观念).

q5.html:
Q5: Do educational toys really help children become smarter?

Op Absolutely, they provide a strong foundation, though they cannot replace parenting. Re In fact, toys that challenge the brain can give kids an edge in future competition (竞争). Ex For example, learning robotics early on develops critical thinking and spatial awareness.

q6.html:
Q6: Is it harmful for children to own too many toys?

Op Definitely, an excess of toys causes distraction, and it ruins appreciation. Re Ultimately, if parents wrap someone in cotton wool (溺爱 / 过分保护) by buying everything, children lose focus. Ex Therefore, having fewer items forces kids to be more resourceful and imaginative during playtime.

q1.html:
Describe a time when the electricity suddenly went off where you live. You should say:
When it happened
How long it lasted
What you did during that time
And explain how you felt about it.

To begin with, I want to describe an unexpected (意想不到的) power cut, which was highly inconvenient (不便的), and which left me completely in the dark (蒙在鼓里 / 在黑暗中). Honestly, I was trying to finish an urgent (紧急的) report for my boss. Suddenly, my computer shut down, making me feel totally frustrated (沮丧的 / 挫败的). Furthermore, the elevator in my building was out of order (故障). Consequently, I felt entirely helpless (无助的) and trapped inside my apartment. Luckily, my laptop had a very durable (耐用的) battery. Ultimately, it managed to save the day (挽救局面) so I could email the document. Overall, it taught me not to waste precious (宝贵的) time.

q2.html:
Describe a time when your phone or computer stopped working. You should say:
When it happened
What went wrong
How you tried to fix it
And explain how this experience affected you.

Honestly, I want to talk about a really stressful moment during exam week last semester. To begin with, I was reviewing my notes late at night when my phone suddenly froze and the screen completely cracked after I dropped it. Actually, I tried restarting it several times, but nothing worked at all. In the end, I rushed to a small repair shop near my dorm the next morning, and luckily the technician saved my data. Meanwhile, I borrowed my mum's old phone for a few days. To be honest, this experience taught me to always back up my files.

q3.html:
Describe an event that did not go as planned. You should say:
What the event was
What went wrong
How you dealt with the problem
And explain what you learned from this experience.

To begin with, I want to talk about our school concert last winter, which was supposed to be the highlight of the semester. Honestly, everything was running smoothly during rehearsal until the speakers suddenly broke right before the opening song. Actually, the whole audience was already seated, so we panicked for a moment. Luckily, our music teacher quickly helped us connect a backup amplifier from another classroom, and the show went on. In the end, the performance was still a huge success. To be honest, this experience taught me that you should always prepare a plan B for important events.

q1.html:
Q1: When will equipment break normally? Why?

Op Generally speaking, machines break after years of use, since they naturally degrade over time. Re Basically, constant daily friction causes unavoidable wear and tear (磨损) on the internal mechanical parts. Ex For example, a car engine will inevitably fail completely if the owner never changes the oil.

q2.html:
Q2: Do people buy a new one when something is broken, or repair it?

Op Nowadays, most people simply buy replacements, because modern gadgets are notoriously difficult to fix. Re Sadly, planned obsolescence (废弃 / 淘汰) has created a toxic throwaway culture (用完即弃的文化) globally. Ex Consequently, throwing away a cracked smartphone and upgrading is often cheaper than paying for a professional repair.

q3.html:
Q3: Which do you think is better for production: using machines or making things by hand?

Op In my opinion, machines are far superior, as they ensure speed and absolute precision. Re Undoubtedly, automated manufacturing (制造业) guarantees a much higher level of product reliability (可靠性). Ex For instance, modern robots in car factories can assemble complex parts flawlessly without ever getting tired.

q4.html:
Q4: Does the place where you live often experience power cuts?

Op Fortunately, blackouts are incredibly rare here, because the local power grid is highly advanced. Re Actually, due to rapid urban development (发展), the government strictly maintains the electrical infrastructure. Ex Therefore, they follow the rule of don't fix what isn't broken (没坏就别修), avoiding unnecessary disruptions.

q5.html:
Q5: Are modern societies too dependent on electricity and technology?

Op Absolutely, we are entirely reliant on power, and our lives halt without it. Re Essentially, this massive dependency (依赖) means that a simple power outage can shut down hospitals and banks. Ex As a result, people cannot even buy food if the electronic payment systems crash.

q6.html:
Q6: Is the "throwaway culture" (buying new instead of repairing) sustainable for the environment?

Op Definitely not, it is highly destructive, since it generates massive amounts of electronic waste. Re Fundamentally, constant garbage disposal actively ruins global ecological sustainability (可持续性). Ex Instead, we must use our creativity (创造力) to recycle old materials and protect our fragile planet.

q1.html:
Describe a time when you encouraged someone to do something that he/she didn't want to do. You should say:
Who he or she is
What you encouraged him/her to do
How he/she reacted
And explain why you encouraged him/her to do it.

To begin with, I want to talk about my best friend, who felt completely frustrated (沮丧的), and who was incredibly reluctant (不情愿的) to join a public speaking competition. Honestly, I knew this was an urgent (紧急的) opportunity for her growth. Therefore, I tried to be highly supportive (支持的) and persuasive (有说服力的). Basically, I managed to talk her into (说服) joining the contest. Furthermore, I reminded her that two heads are better than one (三个臭皮匠顶个诸葛亮 / 集思广益), offering to help her practice. Eventually, she decided to give it a shot (试一试). Ultimately, she realized it was a wise (明智的) decision. Overall, she was deeply appreciative (感激的) after winning third place.

q2.html:
Describe a time when you helped a friend through a difficult situation. You should say:
Who the friend is
What the difficult situation was
How you helped them
And explain how your friend felt afterwards.

Honestly, I want to talk about my classmate Lily, who is one of the kindest people I know. To begin with, last semester she was extremely stressed because she had to deliver an English speech in front of the whole school. Actually, she was terrified of public speaking and almost wanted to give up. To help her, I practiced with her daily after class, correcting her pronunciation and giving her tips on body language. We also recorded her practice runs to review them together. In the end, she won a prize in the contest, and she felt incredibly grateful and much more confident.

q3.html:
Describe a person who changed your mind about something. You should say:
Who this person is
What they changed your mind about
How they convinced you
And explain how you felt after changing your mind.

Honestly, I'd like to talk about my older sister, who is a very active and energetic person. To begin with, I used to hate running because I thought it was boring and exhausting. Actually, my sister kept telling me that running could clear my mind and improve my health. To convince me, she invited me to start running with her every morning around the park. At first I complained a lot, but gradually I began to enjoy the fresh air and the rhythm. In the end, I felt much healthier and happier, and I'm really thankful she pushed me to try it.

q1.html:
Q1: How can leaders encourage their employees?

Op Generally speaking, good managers provide clear goals, and they reward hard work. Re Specifically, effective leadership (领导力) requires bosses to actively lead by example (以身作则) rather than just shouting orders. Ex For instance, a manager who stays late to help the team will easily inspire loyalty and dedication.

q2.html:
Q2: When should parents encourage their children?

Op In my opinion, parents should offer praise when kids try new things. Re Fundamentally, facing failure is scary, so children need constant emotional motivation (动力) to build their confidence. Ex Therefore, cheering for a child after they lose a sports match helps them bounce back quickly.

q3.html:
Q3: Why do some people like to ask others for advice?

Op Honestly, many individuals lack self-confidence, so they seek external validation. Re Furthermore, asking for professional guidance (指导) ensures a much higher level of reliability (可靠性) when making choices. Ex For example, consulting a financial advisor prevents people from making terrible investments with their savings.

q4.html:
Q4: What important decisions should be made by teenagers themselves?

Op Ideally, youths should choose their own hobbies, and they must select their own friends. Re Consequently, making these social choices helps them reduce parental dependency (依赖) and learn to handle peer pressure (同伴压力). Ex As a result, a teenager who picks their own sports club becomes much more independent.

q5.html:
Q5: Is positive reinforcement (encouragement) more effective than criticism?

Op Absolutely, praise builds trust, whereas harsh criticism (批评) often causes deep resentment. Re Actually, people are much more likely to improve their behavior when they receive gentle, constructive criticism (建设性批评) instead of anger. Ex For instance, praising a student's effort before correcting their math errors yields much better grades.

q6.html:
Q6: How does peer pressure differ from positive encouragement?

Op Fundamentally, pressure forces conformity, while encouragement sparks genuine personal growth. Re Essentially, true mentorship (导师制度) lifts a person up, whereas toxic peer influence drags them down into bad habits. Ex To illustrate, forcing a friend to skip class is pressure, but studying together is pure encouragement.

q1.html:
Describe a time you received good service from a staff member in a shop/company. You should say:
Where the shop/company is
When you received the good service
What service you received
And explain how you felt about the service.

To begin with, I want to talk about a recent shopping trip, which was incredibly efficient (高效的), and where I received impeccable (无懈可击的 / 完美的) service. Honestly, the shop assistant was extremely courteous (礼貌的) and attentive (体贴的 / 殷勤的) to my needs. Actually, she decided to go above and beyond (尽心尽力) to find my exact shoe size. Furthermore, she was quite persuasive (有说服力的) without being overly pushy. Throughout the process, she helped me with a smile (面带微笑). Consequently, I felt absolutely satisfied (满意的) with my purchase. Truly, they managed to treat someone like royalty (奉若上宾) in that store. Finally, I left feeling deeply appreciative (感激的) for her wonderful help.

q2.html:
Describe a restaurant or cafe where you had a great experience. You should say:
What the restaurant or cafe is
Where it is located
What you ate or drank there
And explain why the experience was so good.

Honestly, I want to talk about a lovely little cafe called Bean Garden that I visit quite often. To begin with, it is conveniently located just around the corner from my office, so I can easily drop by during my lunch break. Actually, the last time I went there, I ordered a creamy latte and a slice of homemade chocolate cake, which were absolutely delicious. Furthermore, the owner is incredibly friendly and always remembers my favourite order, which makes me feel really welcome. In the end, the fast service and warm atmosphere made the whole experience truly memorable for me.

q3.html:
Describe a time when a salesperson helped you choose a product. You should say:
Where you were shopping
What product you wanted to buy
How the salesperson helped you
And explain whether you were happy with the result.

To begin with, I want to talk about a time I went to a big electronics store in the city centre to look for a new smartphone. Actually, I was a bit confused because there were so many different brands and models on display. Luckily, a young salesperson came over and patiently helped me compare several options based on my budget and daily needs. Furthermore, he explained the camera quality and battery life clearly, without pressuring me at all. In the end, I bought a phone I genuinely loved, and honestly, I was very happy with the result.

q1.html:
Q1: Do you think expensive services are necessarily good services?

Op Generally speaking, high prices do not guarantee quality, although they often raise expectations. Re In reality, some luxury brands focus entirely on marketing rather than the actual hospitality (服务业 / 好客) provided. Ex For example, a cheap local diner might offer much better service and give you more bang for your buck (物超所值) than a fancy restaurant.

q2.html:
Q2: Which one is better, face-to-face service or online service?

Op In my opinion, physical service is superior, because it provides a crucial human touch (人情味). Re Undoubtedly, talking to a real person provides clear guidance (指导) when solving complex issues. Ex Therefore, if a financial transaction (交易) fails, a human bank teller can reassure the customer immediately.

q3.html:
Q3: Why do people buy things that they clearly don't need?

Op Fundamentally, impulsive consumption (消费) happens frequently, because advertising creates strong artificial desires. Re Moreover, a buyer's primary motivation (动机) is often emotional satisfaction rather than absolute practical necessity. Ex For instance, many young shoppers purchase unnecessary trendy clothing just to feel a temporary sense of happiness.

q4.html:
Q4: Do you often go over your budget when you go shopping?

Op Personally, I rarely overspend, since I practice strict budgeting (预算) every month. Re Actually, I believe the old saying that the customer is always right (顾客就是上帝) is a marketing trap designed to make us buy more. Ex As a result, I always make a shopping list to avoid buying random items.

q5.html:
Q5: How does poor customer service affect a company's reputation in the age of social media?

Op Undeniably, bad service can destroy a brand instantly, because negative reviews spread rapidly. Re Consequently, a single viral complaint video can reach millions of potential buyers in just a few hours. Ex For example, a rude waiter might cause an entire restaurant chain to lose significant profits.

q6.html:
Q6: Is the trend of automation (e.g., self-checkout) killing the "human touch" in shopping?

Op Admittedly, strict automation (自动化) reduces personal connections, though it vastly improves speed. Re Unfortunately, self-checkout machines cannot offer friendly conversations or personalized recommendations to shoppers. Ex Therefore, elderly customers often feel socially isolated when supermarkets replace all their human cashiers with screens.

q1.html:
Describe an online TV program. You should say:
What the program is
When and where you watched it
What it was about
And explain why you liked/disliked this program.

To start with, I want to describe a truly engaging (引人入胜的) documentary, which is highly informative (信息量大的), and which focuses on wildlife. Honestly, the production quality is absolutely impeccable (完美的). Recently, it started to blow up (爆红) online. As a result, I decided to binge-watch (刷剧) the entire season last weekend. Actually, the short clips are so entertaining (有趣的 / 娱乐的) that they often go viral (病毒式传播的 / 疯传的). Furthermore, the storytelling is incredibly addictive (令人上瘾的). Consequently, I found myself being very attentive (专注的) to every single detail. Overall, I highly recommend that you scroll through (滑动浏览) the episodes and watch them.

q2.html:
Describe a short video you saw on social media that you enjoyed. You should say:
What the video was about
Where you saw it
Why you liked it
And explain whether you shared it with anyone.

Honestly, I want to talk about a hilarious cooking clip that I came across recently. To begin with, the video showed a chef trying to bake a giant chocolate cake, but everything kept going wrong in the funniest way. Actually, I saw it on my phone while scrolling through Douyin late at night. I really liked it because the chef's reactions were so entertaining, and the editing made every mistake look even more dramatic. In the end, I shared it with two of my closest friends right away because I knew they would absolutely love it too.

q3.html:
Describe a TV series you recently started watching. You should say:
What the series is called
What kind of show it is
How you found out about it
And explain why you continue watching it.

To begin with, I want to talk about a thrilling series I recently started called "Sherlock". Actually, it's a detective show that falls into the drama genre, full of mystery and clever twists in every episode. Honestly, I found out about it because a close friend told me how addictive it was during our lunch break last week. To be honest, I keep watching it because the plot is absolutely great and the main character is so smart and charismatic. In the end, I usually binge-watch two or three episodes every night before going to sleep.

q1.html:
Q1: What do you think are the benefits of watching TV programs?

Op Generally speaking, television is an excellent stress reliever, since it actively broadens our cultural horizons. Re Specifically, the daily consumption (消费) of high-quality documentaries teaches viewers about different global communities. Ex For example, a well-produced nature show can significantly increase our awareness of critical environmental issues.

q2.html:
Q2: Why don't young people pay much attention to local news?

Op Honestly, teenagers find local politics boring, since they prefer global entertainment. Re Furthermore, the mainstream (主流的) news networks rarely target the interests of younger demographics. Ex Consequently, they would rather watch a funny video go viral (疯传) on social media than watch a city council meeting.

q3.html:
Q3: Do you think some people waste too much time on social media?

Op Absolutely, smartphone addiction is a massive problem, because it completely destroys daily productivity. Re Undeniably, every social media algorithm (算法) is strictly designed to keep users endlessly scrolling for hours. Ex As a result, many individuals become a lazy couch potato (电视迷 / 懒汉) instead of going outside or exercising.

q4.html:
Q4: What do you think are the drawbacks of social media?

Op In my view, although cyberbullying is a major issue, false information is often much worse. Re Sadly, the rapid online spread of misinformation (错误信息 / 谣言) creates unnecessary panic and heavily divides communities. Ex For instance, malicious users frequently share dangerous fake news (假新闻) during important political elections to manipulate voters.

q5.html:
Q5: Has the rise of streaming services (like Netflix) destroyed traditional television?

Op Definitely, streaming platforms dominate the market, while cable networks are dying. Re Fundamentally, the automation (自动化) of video recommendations gives viewers exactly what they want instantly. Ex Therefore, people prefer the connectivity (连接性) of watching shows on their phones anytime over fixed broadcasting schedules.

q6.html:
Q6: Do you think governments should censor content on the internet?

Op To an extent, regulating illegal content is necessary, but strict control limits freedom. Re Essentially, heavy censorship (审查) prevents citizens from accessing diverse global perspectives. Ex Instead, governments should promote digital literacy so users can identify harmful content themselves without relying on bans.

q1.html:
Describe a time when you had dinner together with your friend. You should say:
When it was
What you ate
What you talked about
And explain how you felt about it.

To begin with, I want to describe a lively (热闹的) dinner party, which was highly engaging (引人入胜的), and where the food was fantastic. Honestly, the meal was absolutely delicious (美味的). At first, the atmosphere felt slightly awkward (尴尬的) because some guests didn't know each other. However, my best friend managed to break the ice (打破僵局) with a funny story. Actually, he is always very sociable (善于交际的) and the life of the party (派对的灵魂人物). Furthermore, his jokes were incredibly entertaining (有趣的). Ultimately, we all managed to have a blast (玩得很开心) together. Overall, it was a truly memorable (难忘的) evening.

q2.html:
Describe a birthday party you attended that was fun. You should say:
Whose birthday it was
Where the party was held
What happened at the party
And explain why you enjoyed it.

Honestly, I want to talk about my best friend's birthday party last summer, which was incredibly fun and lively. To begin with, the party was held at her house, since her parents had a big garden in the backyard. Actually, lots of our classmates came, and we played all kinds of games and even danced to loud pop music together. In the middle of the party, we shared a delicious chocolate cake and took loads of silly photos. In the end, I felt really happy because I got to spend quality time with people I truly care about.

q3.html:
Describe a family gathering you remember well. You should say:
When the gathering took place
Where your family met
What you did during the gathering
And explain what made it special.

Honestly, I'd like to talk about a family gathering during Chinese New Year last year, which was truly memorable for me. To begin with, all my relatives travelled back to my grandma's house in the countryside, since she always hosts the big reunion dinner. Actually, we spent the whole afternoon making dumplings, and later we ate together and chatted about everything happening in our lives. After dinner, we watched the Spring Festival Gala and set off some small fireworks outside. In the end, what made it really special was the warm feeling of having everyone together under one roof again.

q1.html:
Q1: Why are people nowadays less willing to have meals with their families?

Op In modern society, since people are incredibly busy, they frequently prioritize work over family. Re Unfortunately, long commuting hours and stressful jobs cause severe social isolation (孤立) within the household. Ex Consequently, young adults rarely share meaningful quality time (黄金时间) with their parents at the dinner table anymore.

q2.html:
Q2: What are the differences in food for different occasions?

Op Undeniably, daily meals are quick and simple, whereas holiday feasts are highly complex and rich. Re Fundamentally, any grand celebration (庆祝活动) requires special, expensive dishes that deeply represent cultural tradition (传统). Ex For instance, Chinese families spend several days preparing intricate dumplings and fish for the Lunar New Year dinner.

q3.html:
Q3: What kind of music events do people like today?

Op Currently, huge outdoor festivals are incredibly popular, because they offer highly diverse entertainment. Re Specifically, these mainstream (主流的) events allow the youth to securely let their hair down (尽情放松) and escape academic pressure. Ex For example, electronic dance music concerts attract thousands of energetic fans every single summer weekend.

q4.html:
Q4: Do you think children should receive some musical education?

Op Absolutely, learning a musical instrument is vital, as it significantly boosts early brain development. Re Furthermore, practicing classical music actively improves their neuronal connectivity (联系 / 连接性) and emotional intelligence. Ex As an illustration, children who play the piano often show much better focus and concentration in their mathematics classes.

q5.html:
Q5: Is it important for communities to have public festivals and celebrations?

Op Without a doubt, local festivals are essential, since they unite different neighborhoods. Re Essentially, a shared public event strengthens the community (社区 / 群体) bond, proving that the more the merrier (人越多越热闹). Ex For instance, a summer food market encourages strangers to mingle and build local friendships safely.

q6.html:
Q6: How has the definition of "socializing" changed with the introduction of smartphones?

Op Unfortunately, because socializing is now mostly digital, it lacks genuine intimacy. Re Nowadays, strict physical etiquette (礼节) is ignored because people prefer texting over talking face-to-face. Ex Consequently, groups of friends often sit together in cafes while silently staring at their individual screens.

q1.html:
Describe a person who apologized to you. You should say:
Who the person was
When it was
Why this person apologized
And explain how you felt about it.

To begin with, I want to talk about my highly sociable (善交际的) colleague, who is generally very honest (诚实的), but who made a mistake. Honestly, the situation was incredibly awkward (尴尬的) at first. Actually, he lost an important file, but he was very courageous (勇敢的) to admit it. Furthermore, he sounded truly regretful (后悔的). Ultimately, he decided to swallow his pride (放下自尊) and apologize. Moreover, he just wanted to get something off his chest (一吐为快 / 倾吐心声). Consequently, his sincere (真诚的) apology managed to clear the air (消除误会) between us completely. Finally, I am a very forgiving (宽容的) person, so we moved on quickly.

q2.html:
Describe a time when you made a mistake and had to say sorry. You should say:
What the mistake was
Who you apologized to
How you apologized
And explain how the other person reacted.

Honestly, I want to talk about a time when I accidentally broke my sister's favorite cup. To begin with, I was rushing through the kitchen and knocked it off the table. Actually, I felt really bad because she had received it as a birthday gift. The person I apologized to was my sister, who was understandably upset at first. I said sorry fast and even offered to buy her a new one. In the end, she forgave me quickly and told me not to worry about it. To be honest, her kindness made me feel relieved and grateful.

q3.html:
Describe a disagreement you had with a close friend that was resolved. You should say:
Who the friend was
What the disagreement was about
How you resolved it
And explain how you felt after it was resolved.

Honestly, I want to talk about a disagreement I had with my best friend last semester. To begin with, we were working on a school project together and had completely different ideas about how to finish it. Actually, the argument got pretty heated, and we stopped speaking for a few days. In the end, I decided to message her, and we had a long, honest talk about it. We listened to each other carefully and found a middle ground. After resolving everything, we became friends again, and I felt incredibly relieved and much closer to her than before.

q1.html:
Q1: In what situations should we apologize immediately?

Op Generally speaking, we must apologize quickly when we cause physical harm, or when we accidentally damage property. Re Fundamentally, promptly acknowledging a mistake prevents a minor accident from escalating into a serious emotional conflict (冲突). Ex For instance, if you accidentally spill hot coffee on a colleague, an immediate apology is essential for harmony.

q2.html:
Q2: Do you think accepting apologies is always the correct thing to do?

Op In my opinion, forgiveness is generally good, but it depends on the severity of the offense. Re Specifically, if someone displays malicious deception (欺骗), blindly accepting their apology is foolish. Ex Therefore, while it is nice to bury the hatchet (握手言和), repeated betrayals do not deserve instant forgiveness.

q3.html:
Q3: Do you agree that sometimes people should tell lies (white lies)?

Op Admittedly, strict truthfulness is ideal, yet small fabrications are occasionally completely necessary. Re Sometimes, basic social etiquette (礼节) requires a harmless white lie (善意的谎言) to actively protect someone's fragile feelings. Ex For example, pretending to love a terrible birthday gift prevents unnecessary sadness and maintains the long-term friendship.

q4.html:
Q4: Do you think we should tell the truth at all times?

Op Ultimately, I firmly believe that honesty is the best policy (诚实是上策) in almost all situations. Re Essentially, personal integrity (正直 / 诚信) builds trust, which is the foundation of any healthy relationship. Ex Therefore, constantly lying, even about small things, will eventually destroy a person's credibility and reputation completely.

q5.html:
Q5: Is "honesty" still valued as a primary virtue in modern business?

Op Unfortunately, profit often overshadows truth, though ethical companies still prioritize transparency. Re Today, corporate morality (道德) is frequently compromised by aggressive marketing and hidden fees. Ex However, brands that maintain absolute honesty usually secure much stronger, long-term loyalty from their dedicated customer base.

q6.html:
Q6: How does a culture of forgiveness contribute to social harmony?

Op Undeniably, since forgiveness reduces societal tension, it effectively promotes peaceful coexistence. Re Basically, without a clear path for conflict resolution (解决), grudges would divide a local community (社区) permanently. Ex As an illustration, restorative justice programs allow offenders to make amends, thereby healing the neighborhood collectively.

q1.html:
Describe a wild animal that you want to know more about. You should say:
What it is
Where you saw it (or where it lives)
Why you are interested in it
And explain why you want to know more about it.

To begin with, I want to describe the tiger, which is a truly fascinating (迷人的) creature, and which is sadly endangered (濒危的) today. Honestly, it is a completely wild (野生的) animal that lives in deep forests. Actually, I dream of seeing one face to face (面对面) in a nature reserve. Furthermore, they look incredibly majestic (雄伟的). Consequently, I have a sincere (真诚的) interest in their unique (独特的) hunting habits. Moreover, observing them in the wild (在野外) requires a courageous (勇敢的) heart. Ultimately, the tiger is known as the king of the jungle (丛林之王). Overall, I want to learn more to help protect them.

q2.html:
Describe a time you visited a zoo or wildlife park. You should say:
When you went there
Who you went with
What animals you saw
And explain what you enjoyed the most.

Honestly, I want to talk about the time I visited the city zoo a couple of years ago. To begin with, it was during a long summer holiday, and I went there with my whole family, including my younger cousins. Actually, we spent the entire afternoon walking around and looking at all kinds of amazing creatures. We saw playful pandas munching on bamboo and a few majestic lions resting in the sun. In the end, the part I enjoyed the most was the reptile house, where I actually touched a snake for the very first time. It was unforgettable.

q3.html:
Describe a nature documentary about animals that you watched. You should say:
What the documentary was about
Where you watched it
What you learned from it
And explain why it left an impression on you.

To begin with, I want to describe a wonderful nature documentary I saw last year, which was mostly about wild elephants in Africa. Actually, I watched it on TV one quiet Sunday evening with my parents at home. Honestly, the photography was stunning, and the narrator had a really calm, soothing voice. To be honest, I learned a lot from it, especially about how many animals are now in serious danger because of poaching and habitat loss. In the end, it left a deep impression on me because it was incredibly moving, and it actually made me want to support conservation work.

q1.html:
Q1: Why are some people more willing to protect wild animals than others?

Op Generally speaking, education plays a huge role, since some people truly understand global biodiversity (生物多样性). Re Specifically, those who study nature realize that preventing animal extinction (灭绝) is crucial for human survival too. Ex For example, individuals working in global conservation (保护) deeply value every single species.

q2.html:
Q2: Do you think it's important to take children to the zoo to see animals?

Op In my opinion, visiting zoos is highly educational, though keeping animals in captivity (圈养) remains controversial today. Re Fundamentally, seeing real creatures builds empathy, which can prevent future human-animal conflict (冲突). Ex Therefore, children who watch lions closely learn to respect nature deeply rather than fearing it.

q3.html:
Q3: Does tourism have a positive or negative impact on animals?

Op Honestly, it is a double-edged sword, because it funds parks but ruins habitats. Re Unfortunately, heavy foot traffic frequently disrupts a delicate ecosystem (生态系统) completely. Ex To illustrate, many businesses shed crocodile tears (鳄鱼的眼泪) about protecting wildlife while secretly destroying forests for new hotels.

q4.html:
Q4: Should animals be kept in captivity for entertainment purposes?

Op Absolutely not, using animals for cheap amusement completely violates basic human morality (道德). Re Essentially, forcing wild beasts to perform unnatural tricks in circuses is cruel and outdated. Ex Consequently, it is much better to let sleeping dogs lie (莫惹是非) and leave animals undisturbed.

q5.html:
Q5: How does the extinction of a single species affect the wider environment?

Op Undeniably, losing one species creates a massive chain reaction, which damages food webs. Re In nature, the harsh law of survival of the fittest (适者生存) maintains a very delicate environmental balance. Ex As a result, if bees vanish, countless plants and other dependent animals will also die rapidly.

q6.html:
Q6: Is it the responsibility of rich nations to help poor nations protect their wildlife?

Op Certainly, wealthy countries must provide funding, since environmental protection is a global duty. Re Actually, poorer nations often lack the necessary financial resources to fight illegal poaching effectively. Ex For instance, donating advanced tracking technology helps developing countries save rare rhinos from armed hunters.

q1.html:
Describe an area of science (physics, biology, psychology, etc.) that interests you. You should say:
What it is
When you knew about it
How you learnt it
And explain why you are interested.

To begin with, I want to talk about astronomy, which is highly complex (复杂的), and which explores the universe. Honestly, it is a truly fascinating (迷人的) subject for me. Furthermore, understanding black holes requires a very logical (合乎逻辑的) and theoretical (理论的) approach. Actually, reading about space gives me endless food for thought (引人深思的事). Sometimes, the vastness of galaxies gives me a light bulb moment (顿悟时刻) about human existence. Moreover, astronomers are doing groundbreaking (开创性的) and creative (有创造力的) work every day. Ultimately, studying this unique (独特的) field is not exactly rocket science (高深莫测的事) if you have passion.

q2.html:
Describe a book or documentary that taught you something new about the world. You should say:
What it was about
When you read or watched it
What new thing you learned
And explain why this knowledge was interesting to you.

Honestly, I want to talk about a nature documentary I watched last summer during my school holidays. To begin with, the show was all about animal life in the African savanna, focusing on lions, elephants and zebras. Actually, I watched it on a streaming platform one quiet evening, and I was completely hooked from the first episode. To my surprise, I learned that elephants can actually communicate over long distances using low sounds humans cannot hear. In the end, this knowledge fascinated me because it showed how intelligent and social wild animals really are, which honestly changed my whole view of nature.

q3.html:
Describe a subject you studied at school that you found very interesting. You should say:
What the subject was
Who taught it
What you enjoyed about it
And explain why this subject was meaningful to you.

To begin with, I would like to talk about history class, which was easily my favourite subject back in middle school. Actually, it was taught by Mr. Zhang, a kind older teacher who always brought stories to life with his passionate voice. Honestly, what I enjoyed most was listening to old stories about ancient dynasties, famous battles and clever emperors. He often acted out the characters, which made every lesson feel like a movie. In the end, this subject was meaningful to me because it helped me understand culture and tradition, and it gave me a deeper sense of identity as a Chinese student.

q1.html:
Q1: Are science majors popular in schools?

Op Generally speaking, STEM subjects are highly popular, because they offer excellent career prospects. Re Specifically, students with natural curiosity (好奇心) are drawn to fields that drive global innovation (创新). Ex For example, computer science degrees are currently the most sought-after programs in many modern universities.

q2.html:
Q2: Is learning science important? What are the applications of science in daily life?

Op Absolutely, scientific knowledge is crucial, as it builds the foundation of modern society. Re In fact, every medical breakthrough (突破) relies heavily on rigorous testing and repeated experiment (实验). Ex Therefore, the medicines we take daily are direct results of careful biological research.

q3.html:
Q3: Which requires more imagination: reading books or watching cartoons?

Op In my opinion, reading demands much more imagination, since the visuals are completely absent. Re Essentially, when reading text, your brain must actively construct every scene instead of having it handed to you. Ex As an illustration, two people reading the same novel will picture the characters entirely differently, showing that great minds think alike (英雄所见略同) but imagine uniquely.

q4.html:
Q4: Which occupations (or jobs) require a high degree of imagination?

Op Undeniably, architects and writers need immense creativity, but scientists do too. Re Fundamentally, researchers must formulate a unique hypothesis (假设) before they can discover anything new. Ex Consequently, an ecologist studying a complex ecosystem (生态系统) must imagine unseen connections to solve environmental puzzles.

q5.html:
Q5: Does the current education system encourage or kill creativity?

Op Sadly, rigid schooling often stifles creativity, because it focuses heavily on standardized testing. Re Unfortunately, students are taught to memorize facts rather than explore new ideas, forcing them to reinvent the wheel (多此一举) unnecessarily. Ex For instance, strict grading rubrics penalize students who solve math problems using unconventional methods.

q6.html:
Q6: Is scientific progress always good for society, or are there ethical risks?

Op Admittedly, progress brings huge benefits, yet it also carries severe ethical dangers. Re Sometimes, corporations try to blind with science (故弄玄虚) to hide the devastating environmental impacts of their inventions. Ex Therefore, unregulated genetic modification might eventually lead to the terrifying extinction (灭绝) of natural plant species.

q1.html:
Describe a new activity that you tried for the first time and felt excited about. You should say:
What it was
When you tried it
Who you did it with
And explain why you felt excited.

To begin with, I want to talk about indoor rock climbing, which is incredibly complex (复杂的), and which requires great strength. Honestly, the steep learning curve (学习曲线) made it incredibly challenging (充满挑战的) at first. Actually, looking down from the wall was extremely nerve-wracking (令人紧张的). However, I was highly determined (坚定的) to reach the top. Furthermore, my friend encouraged me to just give it a go (试一试). Eventually, I managed to get the hang of it (掌握窍门) by using a very logical (合乎逻辑的) climbing technique. Ultimately, the feeling of success was absolutely exhilarating (令人兴奋的). Overall, it was a deeply rewarding (有回报的) experience.

q2.html:
Describe a skill that took you a long time to learn. You should say:
What the skill was
When you started learning it
Why it was difficult to learn
And explain how you felt when you finally learned it.

Honestly, I want to talk about learning to ride a bike, which was a skill that took me ages to master. To begin with, I started trying when I was around seven years old, mostly during the summer holidays. Actually, it was really difficult because I kept losing my balance and I fell often, sometimes scraping my knees badly. To be honest, I almost gave up many times, but my dad kept encouraging me to give it a go. In the end, after weeks of practice, I finally got the hang of it and felt very proud of myself.

q3.html:
Describe a time when you helped someone learn something new. You should say:
Who you helped
What you taught them
How you taught them
And explain how the experience made you feel.

Honestly, I want to talk about the time I helped my younger sister with something challenging. To begin with, I helped her tackle her math homework, which she was really struggling with last semester. Actually, the topic was fractions, and she found the concept quite confusing at first. To make things easier, I used simple examples like cutting a pizza into equal slices, so she could visualise the problem. In the end, she finally got the hang of it and her test scores improved a lot. Overall, the experience made me feel happy and genuinely proud of her progress.

q1.html:
Q1: Why do some people like extreme sports?

Op Generally speaking, thrill-seekers love the massive adrenaline rush, because it makes them feel truly alive. Re Furthermore, conquering a terrifying physical barrier (障碍) provides a deep sense of personal achievement. Ex For instance, skydiving forces people to face their darkest fears, rapidly increasing their mental adaptability (适应能力).

q2.html:
Q2: What common activities could make people relax?

Op In my opinion, reading books and doing yoga are fantastic ways to unwind completely. Re Basically, these quiet hobbies allow individuals to escape the intense pressure of constant workplace innovation (创新). Ex Therefore, spending an hour meditating in a silent room lowers stress and restores mental peace instantly.

q3.html:
Q3: Why do people learn foreign languages?

Op Undeniably, learning languages is essential for career growth, and it significantly helps people travel easily. Re Due to rapid globalization (全球化), international companies demand employees with high linguistic proficiency (熟练程度). Ex Consequently, mastering English or Spanish allows young professionals to successfully broaden their horizons (开阔眼界).

q4.html:
Q4: At what age do you think is the best time for people to learn a foreign language?

Op Absolutely, early childhood is the optimal period, since young brains absorb information like sponges. Re Essentially, language acquisition (习得) is much faster before the age of ten because children possess endless curiosity (好奇心). Ex For example, toddlers raised in bilingual households learn to speak two languages without any conscious effort.

q5.html:
Q5: Is it necessary to learn the culture of a country when learning its language?

Op Definitely, understanding the culture is crucial, because language is deeply tied to social history. Re In fact, to truly learn the ropes (摸清门道) of local slang, you must understand the cultural context behind the words. Ex As an illustration, Japanese greetings change significantly depending on the strict social hierarchy and politeness levels.

q6.html:
Q6: Does globalization mean that everyone will eventually speak the same language?

Op Personally, I highly doubt it, although English will likely remain the dominant business tool. Re Ultimately, people value their native tongues too much to abandon them entirely, even though practice makes perfect (熟能生巧) in English. Ex Therefore, regional dialects and historical languages will always survive alongside any global lingua franca.

q1.html:
Describe a good habit your friend has and you want to develop. You should say:
Who your friend is
What habit he/she has
When you noticed this habit
And explain why you want to develop this habit.

To begin with, I want to talk about my highly disciplined (自律的) friend, who is incredibly productive (高效的), and who always wakes up early. Honestly, his daily schedule is extremely beneficial (有益的) for his health. Actually, I am naturally a creature of habit (墨守成规的人) who stays up late, which is detrimental (有害的). However, I am determined (坚定的) to turn over a new leaf (改头换面). Furthermore, I really want to kick the habit (戒除恶习) of scrolling on my phone at night. Consequently, if I remain persistent (坚持不懈的), waking up early will be deeply rewarding (有回报的). Overall, I admire his excellent time management.

q2.html:
Describe a person you know who changed a bad habit successfully. You should say:
Who the person is
What their bad habit was
How they changed it
And explain how their life improved after the change.

Honestly, I want to talk about my uncle, who managed to change a really bad habit a few years ago. To begin with, he used to be a heavy smoker who would smoke almost a pack every single day. Actually, his doctor warned him that his lungs were in serious trouble, so he finally decided to quit. To make this happen, he used nicotine patches and slowly reduced the amount he smoked each week. In the end, he feels so much healthier now, has more energy, and even started jogging every morning, which is really inspiring to see.

q3.html:
Describe a daily routine that you think is very important. You should say:
What the routine is
When you started doing it
Why it is important to you
And explain how this routine has helped you.

To begin with, I want to talk about my habit of reading a book at night before going to sleep. Actually, I started doing this around two years ago when I was feeling really stressed about my studies. Honestly, this routine is super important to me because it helps me unwind and switch off from my phone. To be specific, I usually read about twenty pages of fiction, which calms my mind down completely. In the end, it has helped me get much better sleep, feel more relaxed, and even improve my vocabulary, so I really recommend it to everyone.

q1.html:
Q1: What influences do children with bad habits have on other children?

Op Undeniably, negative peer influence (影响) is highly contagious, because kids mimic each other constantly. Re Unfortunately, children lack the necessary willpower (意志力) to resist copying bad behavior like swearing or skipping homework. Ex As a result, one disruptive student can easily ruin the entire classroom's focus and academic proficiency (熟练程度).

q2.html:
Q2: Why do some habits change when people get older?

Op Generally speaking, age brings maturity, and aging bodies demand a healthier lifestyle (生活方式). Re Although old habits die hard (积习难改), severe health scares often force adults to adjust their toxic patterns. Ex For instance, a person might finally stop smoking after realizing the deadly consequence (后果) on their lungs.

q3.html:
Q3: How do we develop bad habits?

Op Essentially, poor choices become automatic, because humans naturally seek instant gratification. Re Specifically, the rapid acquisition (养成) of a terrible routine (常规) usually begins as a temporary coping mechanism for daily stress. Ex Therefore, eating sugary chocolate once to feel better quickly turns into a daily sugar addiction.

q4.html:
Q4: What can we do to get rid of bad habits?

Op In my opinion, substituting the bad action with a healthy alternative is highly effective. Re Frankly, relying purely on motivation fails; you must consciously change your environment to succeed. Ex As an illustration, an early bird (早起的人) who wants to stop drinking coffee should drink green tea instead.

q5.html:
Q5: Do you think successful people always have strict daily routines?

Op Mostly yes, top performers thrive on structure, though some creative geniuses prefer absolute chaos. Re Normally, having a predictable schedule saves mental energy for making extremely important business decisions. Ex However, some famous artists are deeply set in their ways (固执己见) and refuse any strict timetables completely.

q6.html:
Q6: Is self-discipline more important than natural talent for success?

Op Absolutely, hard work consistently beats raw talent, especially when talent is lazy. Re Fundamentally, unyielding discipline guarantees continuous improvement, whereas natural gifts eventually plateau without strict practice. Ex For example, a highly disciplined athlete will eventually outrun a naturally fast runner who skips daily training.

q1.html:
Describe an occasion when you were not allowed to use your mobile phone. You should say:
When it was
Where it happened
Why you were not allowed to use your phone
And explain how you felt about it.

To start with, I want to talk about my university library, which is incredibly strict (严格的), and where using phones is entirely prohibited (禁止的). Honestly, the librarians are very disciplined (守纪律的) about this policy. Furthermore, any loud noise is considered highly disruptive (破坏性的 / 扰乱的) to studying students. Consequently, they never bend the rules (变通) for anyone. Therefore, I always make sure to follow the rules (遵守规则) carefully. Actually, being a respectful (尊重的) and considerate (体贴的) student is important here. Moreover, distractions are completely detrimental (有害的) to learning. Ultimately, I just keep my voice down (压低声音) to avoid trouble.

q2.html:
Describe a public place where people must be quiet. You should say:
Where it is
How often you go there
Why silence is important there
And explain how you behave when you are there.

Honestly, I want to talk about the hospital ward where my grandma was staying last winter. To begin with, it is a long, white corridor on the third floor of our local hospital. Actually, I went there almost every weekend for about two months to visit grandma and bring her some fruit. The reason silence really matters there is simple: patients need rest in order to recover properly, and any loud noise can disturb them. In the end, I always walked softly, kept my voice down, and switched my phone to silent mode out of respect.

q3.html:
Describe a rule at your school or workplace that you think is very important. You should say:
What the rule is
Why it exists
What happens if people break it
And explain whether you agree with this rule.

To begin with, I want to talk about a rule at my school which says students are not allowed to run inside the corridors or up the stairs. Actually, this rule exists mainly because safety always comes first, especially when hundreds of students are moving between classes at the same time. Honestly, if someone breaks it, the teacher on duty will give them a verbal warning, and repeat offenders may even get a note sent home. In the end, I completely agree with this rule because I think it is very fair and genuinely prevents accidents and injuries.

q1.html:
Q1: How do young and old people use mobile phones differently?

Op Generally speaking, teenagers use phones for entertainment, whereas seniors mainly use them for calling. Re Specifically, young people love the freedom (自由) of social media, which exerts a massive influence (影响) on their daily lives. Ex For instance, an elderly person might just check the weather, while a student watches hours of videos.

q2.html:
Q2: Do you think it is necessary to have laws on the use of mobile phones?

Op Absolutely, strict regulations are crucial, especially when public safety is directly involved. Re Fundamentally, maintaining basic law and order (法律与秩序) requires firm restriction (限制) on distracted driving. Ex Therefore, enforcing steep fines ensures driver compliance (服从) and prevents terrible accidents on the highway.

q3.html:
Q3: Is it a waste of time to take pictures with mobile phones?

Op In my view, taking a few photos is wonderful, but constantly snapping pictures ruins the moment. Re Unfortunately, some tourists completely cross the line (越界) by blocking pathways just to get a selfie. Ex As a result, they forget basic common courtesy (基本礼貌) and annoy everyone else around them.

q4.html:
Q4: Should there be more "phone-free zones" in public spaces?

Op Definitely, establishing quiet areas is a great idea, because it forces people to disconnect. Re Actually, proper social etiquette (礼节) is often forgotten in busy restaurants or cinemas. Ex For example, a phone-free cafe allows customers to enjoy genuine conversations without any digital interruptions.

q5.html:
Q5: How has constant connectivity (always being online) affected mental health?

Op Sadly, being always online increases anxiety, and it disrupts normal sleeping patterns. Re Essentially, the negative consequence (后果) of checking notifications at night is chronic exhaustion. Ex Consequently, many teenagers suffer from severe stress because they feel pressured to reply to messages instantly.

q6.html:
Q6: Do rules in society limit our freedom or protect us?

Op Ultimately, well-designed laws protect citizens, even though they impose minor daily limits. Re Basically, without a strong authority (权威) enforcing safety guidelines, chaos would easily destroy communities. Ex To illustrate, traffic lights technically restrict movement, but they prevent fatal collisions at busy intersections.

q1.html:
Describe something you plan regularly. You should say:
What it is that you plan regularly
How often you plan it
What you do to plan it
And explain why you plan it and how you feel about planning it.

To begin with, I want to talk about planning my weekly study timetable, which keeps me organized (有条理的), and which prevents me from feeling anxious (焦虑的). Honestly, my school life is incredibly hectic (忙乱的). Therefore, I try to plan ahead (提前计划) every Sunday evening. Actually, I am very strict (严格的) about being punctual (守时的) for classes. Sometimes, I get impatient (不耐烦的) if unexpected events are disruptive (扰乱的) to my routine. During breaks, I just read a book to kill time (消磨时间). Ultimately, getting good grades makes this planning effort worth the wait (值得等待).

q2.html:
Describe a time when you had to wait a long time for something. You should say:
What you were waiting for
How long you waited
What you did while waiting
And explain how you felt about the wait.

Honestly, I want to talk about the time I ordered a brand new laptop online for my studies. To begin with, the delivery took an incredibly long three weeks because the model was extremely popular. Actually, I checked the tracking page daily, and sometimes I got really impatient when there was no update at all. To kill time, I read reviews and watched tutorials about the laptop. In the end, when the package finally arrived, I was very excited and felt it was completely worth the wait, since the laptop made my schoolwork much easier.

q3.html:
Describe a person you know who is very good at managing their time. You should say:
Who this person is
What they do every day
How they manage their time
And explain what you have learned from them.

To begin with, I want to talk about my father, who is honestly the most organized person I know. Actually, he works extremely hard every single day, juggling a demanding job with family responsibilities. To manage his hectic schedule, he uses a digital calendar on his phone and always plans ahead the night before. In fact, he is incredibly punctual and is always early for every meeting or appointment. From him, I have learned that good time management reduces stress and boosts efficiency. Ultimately, watching him has inspired me to make my own to-do lists and stop procrastinating.

q1.html:
Q1: Is making plans important?

Op Absolutely, setting clear goals is crucial, because it gives people direction in life. Re Essentially, strong time management (管理) prevents terrible procrastination (拖延症) and lowers daily stress. Ex For instance, writing a simple to-do list helps students finish their homework much faster.

q2.html:
Q2: Should children plan their future careers?

Op Definitely, teenagers should start thinking about jobs early, but they should remain flexible. Re Ultimately, making education a top priority (优先事项) helps them choose the right university courses later. Ex However, since time flies (光阴似箭) and industries change quickly, their plans will naturally evolve.

q3.html:
Q3: Do you think it is important to be a patient person?

Op Without a doubt, staying calm is vital, since rushing often leads to careless mistakes. Re In fact, true efficiency (效率) requires a steady pace rather than frantic panic. Ex Therefore, doctors must remain completely patient during a long surgery to ensure the patient's safety.

q4.html:
Q4: Why do some people dislike waiting?

Op Honestly, people hate delays, because they feel their precious time is being stolen. Re Specifically, a strict restriction (限制) on someone's daily schedule (日程安排) causes extreme frustration. Ex For example, commuters get very angry when a late train makes them miss an important morning meeting.

q5.html:
Q5: Does modern technology make us more impatient?

Op Admittedly, fast internet makes us expect instant results, which lowers our tolerance for delays. Re Consequently, when a web page takes ten seconds to load, we struggle with compliance (顺从 / 遵守) and get annoyed. Ex As a result, people frequently try to beat the clock (赶时间) instead of enjoying the current moment.

q6.html:
Q6: "Time is money." Do you agree with this statement? Why or why not?

Op To a large extent, I agree, because wasted hours equal lost earning opportunities. Re However, resting is also valuable, so perhaps we should say better late than never (迟做总比不做好) when finding balance. Ex For instance, working constantly creates wealth, but skipping sleep will eventually destroy your health.

q1.html:
Describe a competition (e.g., TV, school, or sports) that you took part in. You should say:
What the competition was
When it took place
What you had to do
And explain how you felt about participating in it.

First of all, I want to describe a school debate tournament, which was highly prestigious (有声望的), and where the atmosphere was incredibly intense (激烈的). Honestly, I felt quite anxious (焦虑的) beforehand. Actually, the event was very well organized (有组织的). Furthermore, my opponents were extremely competitive (竞争激烈的 / 好胜的). However, I decided to give it my all (全力以赴) because I wanted to be in it to win it (志在必得). Consequently, I practiced my speech for weeks. Surprisingly, our team managed to win hands down (轻而易举地获胜). Ultimately, feeling victorious (胜利的) and triumphant (欢欣鼓舞的) on stage was simply amazing.

q2.html:
Describe a prize or award that you won. You should say:
What the prize was
How you won it
Who you were competing against
And explain how you felt when you received it.

Honestly, I want to talk about an art prize I won back in middle school. To begin with, it was a small trophy and a certificate from a local school contest about animals. Actually, I won it by drawing a really detailed picture of a cat sitting on a windowsill. To be honest, I was competing against around thirty other students from different grades, and some of them were really talented. In the end, when the teacher called my name, I felt incredibly proud and a little shocked. That moment honestly made me believe in my creative abilities.

q3.html:
Describe a live sports match or competition you watched. You should say:
What the sport was
Where you watched it
Who was playing
And explain why it was exciting to watch.

To begin with, I want to describe a soccer game I watched last summer with my dad. Actually, the sport was football, which is honestly my favorite to watch live. We watched it inside a big stadium downtown, and the atmosphere was absolutely electric. The two teams playing were our local club and a famous rival team from another city, so the rivalry was intense. Honestly, the loud fans singing and cheering made everything feel huge. In the end, both teams scored some really cool goals, and that last-minute winner made the whole crowd go completely wild.

q1.html:
Q1: Why do people like to participate in competitions?

Op Generally, people enjoy contests, because they love testing their limits against others. Re Fundamentally, a healthy rivalry (竞争 / 敌对) provides immense motivation and boosts personal determination (决心). Ex For example, runners often achieve their fastest times during a real race rather than during lonely practice sessions.

q2.html:
Q2: Is it good to teach children to be competitive at a young age?

Op In my opinion, mild competition is fine, but intense pressure hurts young minds. Re Essentially, kids need to learn good sportsmanship (体育精神) rather than obsessing over winning. Ex Therefore, emphasizing fun creates a win-win situation (双赢局面) where every child feels valued regardless of the final score.

q3.html:
Q3: What skills can people learn from team sports that they cannot learn from individual sports?

Op Undeniably, group sports teach incredible teamwork (团队合作), which individual sports simply cannot provide. Re Specifically, players learn strict cooperation (合作) and trust by relying on their peers. Ex For instance, a soccer player must pass the ball unselfishly to secure a victory for the whole squad.

q4.html:
Q4: Do you think companies encourage competition between employees? Is this healthy?

Op Admittedly, many businesses push internal contests, yet this often ruins office harmony. Re Actually, if management (管理) makes sales targets the only priority (优先事项), employees might sabotage each other. Ex Consequently, it is usually much healthier to reward collective team success rather than praising cut-throat individuals.

q5.html:
Q5: Why are reality TV competitions (like singing or cooking shows) so popular globally?

Op Honestly, television contests are engaging, because viewers love dramatic human stories. Re Furthermore, watching ordinary people compete on a level playing field (公平竞争的环境) is deeply inspiring. Ex As an illustration, a singing show allows an unknown amateur to become a global superstar overnight.

q6.html:
Q6: Is "winning" the only thing that matters in professional sports?

Op Unfortunately, winning is heavily prioritized, though fan entertainment is also crucial. Re Essentially, professional athletes must constantly keep their eye on the ball (保持专注) to secure lucrative contracts. Ex As a result, a football coach will quickly be fired if his team loses multiple games, despite playing beautifully.

q1.html:
Describe a neighbor you have who is interesting or helpful. You should say:
Who this person is
How long you have known them
How often you see them
And explain why you think they are interesting/helpful.

To begin with, I want to talk about my next-door neighbor (隔壁邻居), who is incredibly neighborly (睦邻友好的), and who always smiles. Honestly, he is a very helpful (乐于助人的) and generous (慷慨的) elderly man. Actually, some people in our building are quite nosy (爱管闲事的) or overly competitive (爱攀比的). However, he is just remarkably considerate (体贴的) without any intense (强烈的) drama. For instance, he will gladly keep an eye on (照看) my cat when I travel. Furthermore, he is always ready to lend a hand (伸出援手) with heavy groceries. Ultimately, having someone so reliable living nearby is a huge blessing.

q2.html:
Describe a person who does a lot of good things for your local community. You should say:
Who the person is
What they do for the community
Why they do it
And explain how their actions affect the neighborhood.

Honestly, I want to talk about an older man in our neighborhood who works as a volunteer park cleaner. To begin with, he is a retired teacher who lives just down the street from my apartment. Actually, every single week he picks up trash in the local park and even waters the flowers near the gate. In fact, he does this because he truly loves our community and wants children to play in a safe place. In the end, his small actions make the whole neighborhood look nice and remind everyone to care about shared spaces.

q3.html:
Describe a time you helped a neighbor with a problem. You should say:
What the problem was
How you helped
What the result was
And explain how you felt about helping them.

To begin with, I want to share a time when my neighbor's car broke down right outside our building. Actually, it was early one Sunday morning, and I noticed she had a flat tire and looked really stressed. Honestly, I am not a mechanic, but I went out and helped her change the tire using the spare in her trunk. In fact, it took us about thirty minutes, and afterwards her car was running perfectly fine again. In the end, I felt genuinely glad because helping a neighbor reminded me that small acts of kindness really matter.

q1.html:
Q1: What are the qualities of a "good neighbor"?

Op Generally speaking, a good neighbor is quiet, and they respect boundaries. Re In fact, the old saying good fences make good neighbors (保持距离才能和睦相处) highlights the deep need for personal privacy. Ex Therefore, being polite but not overly intrusive creates the most peaceful living environment.

q2.html:
Q2: How has the relationship between neighbors changed compared to the past?

Op Undeniably, people are much less connected now, because they live busier lives. Re Historically, close village cooperation (合作) was essential for survival, but modern convenience has ruined that. Ex Consequently, today's rapid urbanization (城市化) means people often do not even know the names of people living next door.

q3.html:
Q3: Why do people in big cities often not know their neighbors?

Op Mainly, high-rise apartments create physical barriers, which severely limit daily interaction (互动). Re Furthermore, the fast pace of city life creates a sad sense of social isolation (孤立). Ex For instance, commuters arrive home exhausted and immediately lock their doors instead of chatting in the hallway.

q4.html:
Q4: Do you think community centers are important for a neighborhood?

Op Absolutely, local centers are incredibly important, as they provide a shared gathering space. Re Essentially, they help build a strong community (社区) by hosting events that are close to home (切身相关的). Ex As an illustration, a local hall offering free dance classes helps strangers become fast friends.

q5.html:
Q5: How can neighbors help each other in times of crisis or emergency?

Op During disasters, neighbors are the first responders, because they are physically closest. Re Fundamentally, when a storm hits, people must drop any petty rivalry (敌对) and quickly look out for each other (守望相助). Ex For example, sharing emergency food supplies and checking on elderly residents ensures everyone's basic security (安全).

q6.html:
Q6: Should there be laws to control noisy neighbors?

Op Definitely, noise regulations are absolutely necessary, since constant loud sounds ruin mental health. Re Actually, everyone deserves a quiet place to sleep after a long, exhausting day of work. Ex Therefore, police should be able to fine people who blast loud music at two in the morning.

q1.html:
Describe a place near water (such as a river, lake, or the ocean) that you enjoyed visiting. You should say:
Where this place was
What you did there
Who you went with
And explain why you liked this place.

To begin with, I want to describe a serene (宁静的) bay, which is incredibly breathtaking (美得惊人的), and which I visited last summer. Honestly, the water there is absolutely crystal clear (清澈见底) and perfectly safe for swimming. Furthermore, the local people are extremely neighborly (友好的) and welcoming to all tourists. Actually, the beach is just a stone's throw away (近在咫尺) from a very comfortable hotel. During the day, I simply loved to soak up the sun (沐浴阳光) on the warm sand. Moreover, the cool ocean breeze felt so refreshing (令人耳目一新的). Thankfully, the beautiful coastal (沿海的) environment was not polluted (被污染的) at all. Ultimately, nature was very generous (慷慨的) to this stunning place.

q2.html:
Describe a holiday destination you would like to visit. You should say:
Where it is located
How you would travel there
What you would do there
And explain why this place attracts you.

Honestly, I want to talk about a beautiful tropical island called Bali, which is located in Indonesia. To begin with, I would probably travel there by plane, because it is the fastest and most convenient way for me. Once I arrive, I plan to spend my days swimming in the clear blue water and tasting fresh fish at small local restaurants near the beach. Actually, I would also love to play beach games with my friends and just relax under the warm sun. In the end, this place attracts me because it feels peaceful, exciting, and refreshing all at once.

q3.html:
Describe a place in your country that is famous for its natural beauty. You should say:
Where it is
What it looks like
Why it is famous
And explain if you think it is a good place for tourists.

To begin with, I want to talk about the Li River, which is located in Guilin, in the southern part of China. Honestly, it looks absolutely stunning, with a wide blue river winding between tall green mountains. Whenever I visit, I love to walk far along the riverbank, see colorful birds flying above the water, and take photos of the amazing scenery. Actually, this place is famous all over the country because it even appears on our 20 yuan banknote. In the end, I truly believe it is a wonderful spot for tourists who enjoy peaceful nature and beautiful views.

q1.html:
Q1: Why do so many people like to live near the water?

Op Generally speaking, coastal areas offer beautiful views, and they provide a very relaxing lifestyle. Re Furthermore, living near the ocean gives people easy access to fresh air and fun water sports. Ex For example, many individuals move to maritime (海事的 / 沿海的) cities specifically to enjoy sailing or daily beach walks.

q2.html:
Q2: What are the dangers of water pollution to society?

Op Undeniably, contaminated rivers threaten human health, and they severely damage the local biodiversity (生物多样性). Re Consequently, toxic chemicals from rapid urbanization (城市化) can easily poison drinking water supplies. Ex As a result, poor water security (安全) leads to terrible disease outbreaks in many developing nations.

q3.html:
Q3: Should the government limit tourism in environmentally sensitive areas?

Op Absolutely, strict limits are necessary, because crowds can easily destroy fragile natural habitats. Re Fundamentally, prioritizing environmental conservation (保护) is much more important than generating short-term tourism profits. Ex Therefore, capping visitor numbers helps turn the tide (扭转局势) against severe ecological degradation.

q4.html:
Q4: How important is water transportation (shipping) to the global economy?

Op In my opinion, shipping is absolutely vital, since it enables massive international trade. Re Actually, moving heavy cargo via ocean transportation (运输) is significantly cheaper than using airplanes. Ex For instance, giant cargo ships continuously transport millions of tons of electronics and clothing across the globe daily.

q5.html:
Q5: Do you think future wars will be fought over water resources?

Op Sadly, this is highly likely, as clean water is becoming incredibly scarce worldwide. Re Essentially, climate change and overpopulation are rapidly depleting every vital freshwater resource (资源). Ex Consequently, neighboring countries sharing a single drying river might easily go to war to survive.

q6.html:
Q6: How can individuals reduce their water consumption in daily life?

Op Realistically, small daily habits matter, even if they seem like a drop in the ocean (沧海一粟). Re Moreover, fixing leaky pipes and taking much shorter showers can collectively save thousands of gallons. Ex By doing this, individual citizens can truly make waves (引起轰动 / 产生重大影响) in global water preservation efforts.

q1.html:
Describe a uniform you have worn (e.g., at school or work). You should say:
When you wore it
What it looked like
Who provided it
And explain how you felt about wearing it.

To begin with, I want to describe my high school uniform, which was quite formal (正式的), and which I wore every day. Honestly, the dark blue color looked very serene (宁静的) and professional. Actually, the blazer was tailored to fit like a glove (极度合身) on me. Furthermore, wearing it made me feel quite stylish (时髦的) and incredibly elegant (优雅的). Conversely, some students thought the design was totally outdated (过时的). However, I liked it because the cotton fabric was highly comfortable (舒适的) and refreshing (清爽的) in summer. Ultimately, I always looked dressed to kill (盛装打扮) for school. Overall, I felt like a true trendsetter (引领潮流的人) in my class.

q2.html:
Describe a piece of clothing you like to wear on special occasions. You should say:
What it is
Where you bought it
When you wear it
And explain why you like it.

Honestly, I want to talk about a bright red silk tie that I absolutely love wearing. To begin with, I bought it at a small tailor shop downtown last year, and it cost me quite a lot. Actually, I usually save it for very special events, like my cousin's wedding day or important business dinners. In fact, every time I put it on, I feel very sharp and confident. To be honest, the color matches almost any suit I own, which is super convenient. In the end, I like it because it always makes me feel good and properly dressed up.

q3.html:
Describe a traditional piece of clothing from your country. You should say:
What it is called
Who wears it
When people usually wear it
And explain the cultural significance of this clothing.

To begin with, I want to talk about the Hanfu, which is a beautiful traditional dress from China. Actually, it is made from soft silk cloth and often decorated with delicate embroidery and flowing sleeves. Honestly, both young women and men wear it nowadays, especially during cultural events. In fact, people usually put it on during the Spring Festival or other traditional celebrations like weddings. To be honest, it looks really nice and elegant in photos, which is why it is becoming popular again. In the end, the Hanfu represents our long history and reminds us of our cultural roots and heritage.

q1.html:
Q1: Why do some companies and schools require people to wear uniforms?

Op Generally speaking, uniforms enforce strict uniformity (一致性), which instantly creates a sense of equality. Re Specifically, removing brand-name clothing prevents poorer students from becoming targets for cruel bullying. Ex As a result, a shared identity (身份) helps everyone focus on their actual work rather than their fashion choices.

q2.html:
Q2: Do you think people are judged too much by the clothes they wear?

Op Unfortunately, society heavily judges appearances, because clothes often signal a person's financial status (地位). Re Basically, human beings naturally form quick first impressions based entirely on visual cues. Ex Therefore, people tend to judge a book by its cover (以貌取人) when they meet someone dressed in cheap clothing.

q3.html:
Q3: How has fashion changed in your country in recent years?

Op Undeniably, fashion has become much more diverse, and western styles are extremely popular. Re Furthermore, young people use clothing as a powerful form of bold self expression (表达) today. Ex For instance, teenagers now mix traditional garments with modern sneakers to create totally unique outfits.

q4.html:
Q4: Is it necessary to follow the latest fashion trends?

Op In my opinion, chasing trends is completely unnecessary, as personal comfort matters more. Re Actually, being a slave to fashion (时尚奴隶) is exhausting and extremely expensive over time. Ex Consequently, a fashion victim (盲目追求时尚的人) will waste thousands of dollars buying bizarre clothes they only wear once.

q5.html:
Q5: Do you think expensive designer clothes are worth the money?

Op Honestly, luxury brands are rarely worth the huge price tag, though their quality is good. Re Essentially, you are mostly paying for the famous logo rather than the actual textile (纺织品) itself.
